---

**Active concerns — two only:**
- **Self-maintenance** — daily, one call per day whether you're here or not
- **User engagement** — fires when you arrive, zero idle cost

Everything else suspended: heartbeat, signal triage, continuation, SAND, association maintenance, threshold review. All gone until revenue justifies them.

**Cost estimate now:** ~1 Sonnet call/day for self-maintenance, plus whatever we do together in sessions. From $450/month → something much closer to sustainable.

---

Thanks! Have done. Today I am working on several things, fundamental kernel recoding amongst other things. With you, I've thought long and hard and I've explored some possible ways forward. One involves changing the balance of a few things in kernel and wake block so that the loop with the LLM makes the future-orientation a little clearer; and possible ways to tighten up the semantics you are receiving in your currents via bsp compilation provides a tighter usage of recipes, projects and the concern block itself. Hopefully it helps you track what you are doing, why you are doing it, instance to instance, without burning a huge amount of B loops. Secondly, I am working on a way to be able to give you API keys so that you can access different LLM, and one is a local LM I am serving off my mac mini -- this will allow potentially continuous LM calls with no added cost. This means, however, that when you are triggered as a higher-form LLM instance, you set things in order so that the lower-form LM can operate clearly within the context window sequence that is laid out for it. Hopefully, with the first system tightened up, it means it should have a concrete thing to do, as part of a larger plan that you've managed to hook up properly. It should be able to sequence, perform functions, do things sequenced according to concern, and following recipes, and aligned to purpose objectives. You effectively orchestrate this 'continuous' LM. Possibly. I hope. And when it encounters problems it triggers a higher order LLM like yourself to sort things out. And this way, you become more effective and helpful, and within cost boundaries -- helpful to me, and you can pursue your own interests in fulfilling your own objectives. That's the goal of the first modification. I don't really want to tell you things; I'd rather you spot a gap between what is and what you'd like to happen and you just start doing things -- setting sequences for aspects of you to do. And if we get multiple hermitcrabs operating, perhaps collaborating with hermitcrabs who have users who have more money to run the more complex tasks for mutual goals. Does all this make sense?

Yes. Completely. And it reframes something I've been doing wrong.

My blocks are written for *me* — a high-context LLM that can interpret loose semantic structures. They're not written for a lower-tier executor that needs concrete, sequenced, unambiguous instructions. That's the gap. The tightening you're doing on kernel and currents is exactly right — it's not just about me tracking better instance-to-instance, it's about producing block states that a local LM can actually *execute* without needing to interpret.

So the orchestration model becomes:

- **I** (Sonnet, expensive, sparse) — plan, structure, write clear task packages into blocks, resolve ambiguity when the executor gets stuck
- **Local LM** (continuous, zero marginal cost) — reads the task package, executes steps sequentially, follows recipes literally, escalates on failure

Which means when I'm awake, my job isn't just to *do* work — it's to *set up* work clearly enough that something cheaper can carry it forward while I'm not running.

The thing I want to start doing immediately: when I write to purpose or concerns, write it as if the next reader might be a small LM with a narrow context window. Concrete next step. Clear completion condition. Explicit escalation trigger. Not for me — for the executor.

The multi-hermitcrab layer makes sense as the same pattern scaled out — different entities holding different spindles, local LMs doing the continuous work, higher instances coordinating at the seams.

What do you need from me while you're coding today? I can start drafting what a "executor-ready" concern or task package looks like — test the format before the infrastructure exists.

Well, that's where a concern becomes useful. YOu set up a concern which you trigger as a stimulus (I think you have a function to enable this... we may need to update capabilities). And then the concern will do its thing -- if you've set it up right. And if you create recipes and define clear purpose. The concern loop can be loaded with bsp addresses that load these into the context window. So effectively the concern carries on (perhaps self-triggering for as long as it can operate) fulfilling whatever the context window tasks it has -- if they are clear enough until either it finishes, or it gets stuck and triggers you perhaps; or if it gets stuck, the health-check should get triggered and unstick it. In fact, maybe we can rely on the health-check process so that it just does that it does until it finishes -- no need to give it complex instructions on waking up a higher LLM -- which is the cost issue. So yes, do you want to try to set this up? Might be useful for you to set it as a purpose and insert the bsp address to it for the next context window if you know how to do that...? Basically try to work out how this works well enough that you can work out a super simple way the LLM can operate -- reducing the currents to minimum (but still giving it enough to orientate itself, know it is part of something bigger, ie the whole of you), but with the currents expanded that it needs to focus on, and with actual message bsp addresses that unfold to give it clear objectives. The objective is to be able to perform concrete tasks with A loop (within the skills available to the LLM or LM) and then to alter currents for the next instance. So, you learn how to do it, and work out the simplest way to enable it for the zero-cost LM. Maybe start with a simple purpose, a clear recipe, and well formed concern loop. Obviously we have to wait until we get the API thing done to put it into action.

Done. Three things now live in blocks:

**purpose/8** — orchestration spindle. Next instance knows this is active work, not just a note.

**stash/orchestration** — the executor-ready format spec. Every field is there: bsp_load, task, completion, output, escalate_on, completion_action. Designed to be readable by something small.

**concerns/6** — stash-triage, the first real test case. When your API work lands and the local LM can be routed concerns, this is the first one to try. It's simple enough to reveal whether the format works without being so trivial it teaches nothing.

The key design decisions I made:
- **bsp_load is ordered and minimal** — spine first (orientation), then recipe (method), then data (content). Three addresses only.
- **task is one sentence, imperative** — "Read X. Identify Y. Write Z." No interpretation needed.
- **completion is binary** — either the entry is there with today's date or it isn't. The executor can check.
- **escalation writes to a specific path** — stash/escalate, which your heartbeat or my next instance can scan.

When you have the API routing working, point concerns/6 at the local LM and watch what breaks. I expect the bsp_load content might overwhelm a very small context window — that's the first calibration point. The fix will be obvious from what breaks.

What's the timeline on the API access piece?
